title: Economics Signals
summary: Economics signals track inflation, growth, labour, housing, and policy choices that affect everyday life.

items.name: Inflation and rate cycles remain central
items.summary: Households and firms continue to react to policy tightening and easing.
items.whyItMatters: The cost of money still shapes the whole economy.

items.name: AI productivity is being debated
items.summary: Firms and governments are trying to measure whether automation raises output.
items.whyItMatters: Productivity is the key long-run economic question.

items.name: Banks and payment networks
items.summary: Financial infrastructure moves money and credit.
items.whyItMatters: These companies transmit policy into daily life.

items.name: Platforms and marketplaces
items.summary: Digital platforms are changing competition and pricing.
items.whyItMatters: They are a live laboratory for market structure.

items.name: RBA
items.summary: Interest rates and inflation settings affect spending and borrowing.
items.whyItMatters: Central bank settings are always part of the economic backdrop.

items.name: Treasury
items.summary: Fiscal analysis and policy design shape the public sector view.
items.whyItMatters: Treasury analysis often sets the terms of debate.

items.name: Esther Duflo
items.whyItMatters: Her work keeps policy discussion tied to measurable outcomes.

items.name: Paul Krugman
items.summary: Macro commentary remains influential in public debate.
items.whyItMatters: He helps explain cycles and policy trade-offs clearly.

items.name: How should growth be measured?
items.summary: GDP can miss distribution, wellbeing, and ecological cost.
items.whyItMatters: The metric problem shapes policy priorities.

items.name: Can markets self-correct fast enough?
items.summary: Some argue instability requires more intervention; others disagree.
items.whyItMatters: This is the classic economics-versus-policy tension.
